design_philosophy.core_loop: Command followers to build structures and train units, cast spells to reshape terrain, eliminate enemy tribes through overwhelming force or strategic spell use
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing direct unit command (RTS elements) with terrain manipulation and spell casting (god game elements); managing mana generation through follower population growth
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embodying a shaman leading her tribe to supremacy across a solar system, learning new spells, and ultimately transcending to godhood
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radical departure from previous Populous games by replacing omnipotent god gameplay with a direct shaman commander role. Hybrid design attempting to merge real-time strategy with god game mechanics. No formal resource management except mana and wood—emphasizes action and spell use over economic simulation. Critics noted this identity split weakened both game modes.

difficulty.lives_system: Shaman can be reincarnated if killed, preserving player agency
difficulty.checkpointing: Campaign spans 25 missions with progression across planetary system
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Spell acquisition and follower unit composition decisions affect challenge; later levels place all enemy shamans on same world, increasing complexity

progression.upgrade_system: Spells learned throughout campaign via vault entry (permanent) or totem worship (limited use). Twenty-six total spells progressively unlocked. Shamans gain combat and casting capabilities.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Progression through 25 campaign missions across solar system; planetary sequence determines spell and enemy availability; optional wildmen conversion via Convert spell expands follower base
replayability_hooks: Multiplayer support for up to 4 players via LAN, modem, or Internet
Multiple unit types (Braves, Warriors, Firewarriors, Preachers, Spies) enable tactical variation
Spell loadout customization based on vault discoveries
Variable map fog of war affects information strategy

world_building_techniques: Tribe color coding (Matak-green, Chumara-yellow, Dakini-red, player-blue) establishes faction identity
Planetary system exploration reinforces journey toward godhood
Shaman-led tribal conflict frames progression as ascending hierarchy
Wildmen as neutral environmental feature creates dynamic encounter zones
Female shaman protagonist subverts traditional god-game gender roles

boss_design.philosophy: Enemy shamans serve as faction leaders with unique magical abilities and reincarnation mechanic mirroring player shaman. Later missions converge multiple shamans on single worlds, creating escalating multi-faction conflicts.
notable_bosses: Enemy shamans (Matak, Chumara, Dakini) with spell-casting and terrain manipulation abilities
Final objective: defeat all enemy shamans to achieve player shaman godhood

faction_indicators: Four tribal colors establish clear team/faction alignment
Female shaman leadership centralizes tribal identity
Shamanic spellcasting creates magical doctrine differentiation
danger_signals: Enemy shaman spell casting (terrain deformation, unit swarms)
Convergence of multiple hostile shamans on single map
environmental_storytelling: Terrain topology and deformation show previous magical conflicts
Tree and water clustering indicates wildmen population zones
Structures and building placement reflect tribal territorial control
Planetary progression implies cosmic-scale conflict narrative
